hero.label: Secure Data Transfer
hero.title:
Move data <1>directly</1>.
No cloud.
No middleman.
hero.description: Mirall is built for very large files. Move hundreds of gigabytes or even terabytes straight between devices: create a space, invite the people who need the data, and it travels device to device, encrypted end-to-end, with no cloud in the middle.
hero.betaNotice: Mirall is currently in beta. Some features may change and you might encounter bugs.
hero.industries: Designed for hundreds of gigabytes to terabytes. No size caps, no per-GB fees.

features.title: Built different
features.description: No accounts. No uploads to someone else's server. Mirall gives you a private space to share files the way it should work.

spaces.title: Shared Spaces
spaces.description: Create topic-based spaces for your team, project, or group. Everyone in a space sees all available files and chooses what to download.

inviteOnly.title: Invite-Only Access
inviteOnly.description: A space is reachable only by invite link — and by default the link isn't enough on its own: each person asks to join and you approve them. Switch on auto-approve and anyone holding the link walks straight in.

folders.title: Files or Whole Folders
folders.description: Share single files, or a whole folder that keeps itself in sync as you work. Others can browse it and take what they need, or mirror it to their own device as a live copy that follows every change.

noLimits.title: No Limits, No Meter
noLimits.description: Move a 500 GB master as easily as a PDF. No size caps and no per-GB bill — ever. Transfers pause and resume, pick themselves back up when the other person returns, and every finished file is checked against the original.

direct.title: Direct Device-to-Device
direct.description: Files move straight between devices over encrypted connections, served in place from the originals. No server ever sees your data, because there is no server.

encrypted.title: Encrypted by Default
encrypted.description: Every connection between devices is end-to-end encrypted, with no keys to manage. On your device, Mirall encrypts its own records — which spaces you're in, who's in them, and what you share — and keeps your identity key in the system keychain, so a stolen laptop reveals none of it.

howItWorks.title: How it works
howItWorks.description: Three steps. No setup wizards, no admin panels.

items.title: Create a space
items.description: Name it, pick an icon, and you're live. A space is a private group where you and the people you approve share files.

items.title: Invite your people
items.description: Create an invite link and send it. They ask to join, you approve — and they're connected directly to you, no server in between.

items.title: Share and download
items.description: Drop files into the space. Everyone sees what's available. Each person downloads exactly what they need.

faq.title: FAQ
faq.description: Everything you need to know about Mirall.

items.question: Where are my files stored?
items.answer: On your device, and only your device — right where they already are. Sharing a file leaves it where it is on your disk; other members download it directly from you, and there is no cloud server and no intermediary storage in between. Once someone downloads a file, they have their own local copy.

items.question: Do both of us need to be online at the same time?
items.answer: Yes. Files move directly between devices, so the person sharing a file has to be online and running Mirall while you download it — there's no server holding a copy in the middle. You don't have to sit and watch it, though: if either of you drops off, the transfer pauses and picks up where it left off the next time you're both online.

items.question: What happens when I'm offline?
items.answer: Files you've already downloaded stay on your device — they're yours, and they work offline. Other members can still see what you've shared, but they can't download it from you until you're back. Downloading a file doesn't make you a second source for it either: the member who shared a file is the one who serves it, so while they're offline it's marked “Owner offline” and waits for them.

items.question: Is it really encrypted?
items.answer: Yes. Every connection between devices is end-to-end encrypted, so nobody can see your files or metadata in transit, and there's no central server that could be breached. On your own device, Mirall's records are encrypted at rest and your identity key is wrapped using your operating system's keychain, so copying Mirall's data folder gives up neither.

items.question: How do I invite someone to a space?
items.answer: A space gives you an invite link. Getting it to the other person is up to you — Mirall never sends it on your behalf, so pass it through a channel you trust, ideally an end-to-end encrypted messenger. Treat the link like a key: anyone holding it can ask to join, and it works until the expiry you chose. Approval is the backstop — by default you still let each person in yourself, so a link that gets forwarded doesn't quietly hand someone access.

items.question: What languages does Mirall support?
items.answer: Five: English, German, French, Spanish, and Italian. Mirall picks up your OS language on first launch, and you can switch at any time.

items.question: Are there file size limits?
items.answer: No. Mirall is built for the files that break everything else — hundreds of gigabytes, or a single file into the terabytes, transfer like any other. Content is streamed in chunks rather than uploaded as a whole, there are no size caps, and there's no per-GB bill. Speed depends on your network and the sender's upload bandwidth.

items.question: What happens if a large transfer is interrupted?
items.answer: It carries on where it stopped. Mirall verifies each chunk as it arrives and keeps a record of your progress, so a dropped connection, a closed laptop, or the sender going offline costs you the interruption — not the transfer. You can pause and resume by hand, and when a file finishes, Mirall confirms its contents match the sender's original exactly.

items.question: Is Mirall free?
items.answer: Mirall is free for personal use, and we plan to offer additional paid services in the future.

items.question: How do I keep Mirall up to date?
items.answer: You don't have to. Once installed, Mirall updates itself in the background and the new version is live the next time you launch — no reinstalls, no installer downloads.

download.label: Download
download.title: Download Mirall
download.description: Available for macOS, Windows, and Linux. Pick your platform and start moving data directly between devices in seconds. Once installed, Mirall keeps itself up to date — new versions arrive in the background.
download.betaNotice: This is a beta release — we are looking for your feedback.

preview.label: Preview
preview.title: Mirall preview build
preview.description: Internal staging channel for testing the next Mirall release before it ships to production. Builds here are signed and installable, but may contain unfinished features or bugs.
preview.warning: Preview builds are pre-release and may be unstable. Don't install on a machine you depend on.

cta.title: Your files. Your devices. No middleman.
cta.description: Join the movement that share files the way it should be — direct, encrypted, and free from cloud lock-in.

footer.tagline: Secure data transfer for teams. No cloud, no accounts, no compromises.

support.title: Support
support.heading: Get help from inside the app
support.description: Mirall has built-in support. Click the "Send Feedback" button in the top right corner of the app to report issues, request features, or ask questions. Add your email if you'd like a reply, and Mirall attaches version and platform diagnostics so we can debug faster. Your message goes directly to our team.
support.screenshotAlt: Mirall app with the Send Feedback button highlighted

steps.title: How to reach us
items: Open Mirall on your desktop
Click the "Send Feedback" button in the top right corner
Describe your issue or question — add your email if you want a reply
Hit send — we'll get back to you

support.note: No external ticketing system, no email chains. Everything stays in the app.

hub.label: Documentation
hub.heading: Mirall documentation
hub.intro: Mirall is a secure data transfer app for desktop. No cloud, no accounts — files move directly between you and the people you invite. The docs are organized into four kinds of material, so you can find what fits the moment you're in.

cards.title: Tutorials
cards.description: New to Mirall? Start here. Guided, end-to-end lessons that get you to a first success — sending files and sharing a folder.

cards.title: How-to guides
cards.description: Know what you want to do? Step-by-step recipes for a single task — create a space, approve who joins, mirror a folder, and more.

cards.title: Reference
cards.description: Need the facts? Complete tables of file statuses, folder badges, keyboard shortcuts, settings, and the application menu.

cards.title: Explanation
cards.description: Want to understand the why? How peer-to-peer works, the privacy and approval model, mirroring, and how Mirall uses your disk.

tutorials.label: Tutorials
tutorials.heading: Tutorials
tutorials.intro: Guided lessons for newcomers. Follow them top to bottom — each one takes you all the way through a real task so you learn Mirall by doing.

items.title: Send your first files
items.intro: In a few minutes you'll install Mirall, create a space, invite someone, approve them, and watch a file travel directly from your device to theirs. You only need a second person with Mirall installed to receive — or a second computer of your own.

items.title: Install and open Mirall
items.text: Download Mirall for your platform from the download page and open it. The first time it launches, Mirall asks for a **display name** and an optional **avatar** — that's how other members will see you. Your OS language is detected automatically.

items.title: Create a space
items.text: On the **Shared Spaces** screen, click **Create Space**, give it a name, pick an icon, and click **Initialize Space**. Mirall confirms with **Space Created** — click **Done**. A space is a private group where you and the people you approve share files.

items.title: Create an invite link
items.text: Open the space and click **Invite**. Choose whether to **auto-approve** anyone who uses the link, pick how long it stays valid — **2 hours**, **2 days**, or **2 weeks** — and click **Create invite link**. Copy the link and send it however you like: chat, email, anything.

items.title: Let them join
items.text: The other person clicks **Join Space**, pastes the link, and confirms. Unless you turned auto-approve on, they now wait: their screen reads **Waiting to be let in**, and they can't see anything shared in the space yet.

items.title: Approve them
items.text: Back in your space, a banner appears reading **“<name> wants to join”**. Click **Approve**. They're admitted, the space contents open up to them, and you both appear in each other's **Members** panel.

items.title: Share a file
items.text: Drag a file onto the **Drop to Share** zone in the sidebar, or use **Share…** to pick one. Mirall offers it to the space straight from the original on your disk — it never makes a second copy.

items.title: Download on the other side
items.text: On the other person's Mirall, the file appears with a download button. They click it and the file transfers directly from your device to theirs, with live progress, speed, and time remaining. When it lands, a green shield confirms the contents match your original exactly. No server ever touched the file.

blocks.alt: Mirall's welcome screen asking for a display name and an optional avatar before first use
Mirall's Invite to Space dialog with an auto-approve toggle and a link expiry choice of 2 hours, 2 days, or 2 weeks
A Mirall space showing a “wants to join” banner with Approve and Deny buttons above the shared files
A Mirall space with shared files, the Drop to Share zone, and the Members panel in the sidebar

blocks.text: Nothing downloads automatically. Members always see what's available and choose what to pull onto their own device.

items.title: Share a folder and keep it in sync
items.intro: Instead of picking files one by one, you can share a whole folder into a space and keep it in sync. This lesson walks you through sharing a folder, then having another member mirror it to their own disk.

items.title: Open a space
items.text: Open an existing space, or create one as in the first tutorial. Anything you share lives inside a space.

items.title: Add a folder
items.text: Open **Add Folder** (from **Share…** in the sidebar, the **File** menu, or `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + U`). Pick a folder on your computer and give it a **share name** — that's how the folder appears to other members. Subfolders are included automatically.

items.title: Check the preview
items.text: Click **Next: Preview**. Mirall scans the folder in the background and shows you what it's about to share — how many files and how large. Click **Add Folder** to confirm.

items.title: Watch it publish
items.text: Mirall starts offering the folder's files to the space, serving them straight from the originals on your disk. From now on, when you add, edit, or remove files in that folder on your computer, the change syncs to the space on its own.

items.title: Have a member mirror it
items.text: Another member opens the shared folder. They can **browse** it — open subfolders and download individual files — or use **Mirror to Disk…** to keep a live, always-up-to-date copy in a folder of their choosing.

items.title: Watch it stay in sync
items.text: Change a file in your shared folder. On the mirroring member's computer, the mirror updates to match. The mirror is **read-only**: if they edit a mirrored file, it's restored to your version on the next sync.

blocks.alt: Mirall's Add Folder dialog with a folder path, a share name field, and a Next: Preview button
A Mirall space with a shared Production Footage folder card marked “Shared by you”

blocks.text: Sharing a folder never doubles its disk usage — Mirall serves the files in place, from the originals.

guides.label: How-to guides
guides.heading: How-to guides
guides.intro: Task-focused recipes. Each one assumes you already know the basics and gets you straight to a single goal.

items.text: Click **Create Space** on the Shared Spaces screen.
Give the space a name and pick an icon.
Click **Initialize Space**. Mirall confirms with **Space Created**.
Click **Done**. You now have a space to yourself — invite people from inside it.

blocks.text: Creating a space doesn't hand you an invite. Open the space and use **Invite** when you're ready to add someone.

items.text: Click **Join Space** on the Shared Spaces screen.
Paste the invite link you received. Both the `mirall://join/…` link and the bare code work.
Optionally give the space a local name — only you will see it.
Click **Join Space**.

blocks.text: Unless the invite was set to auto-approve, joining is a **request**: your screen reads **Waiting to be let in** until an existing member approves you. You can leave the window open — you'll be admitted automatically as soon as a member is online, and you don't need to be online at the moment they approve. Until then the space's contents stay closed to you, and the only action available is **Cancel request**.
Once someone approves you, the space opens and its files appear. If the invite had **auto-approve** turned on, you skip the wait entirely and land straight in.
An invite link has an expiry. If yours has lapsed, Mirall tells you the link has expired — ask the person who sent it for a new one.

image.alt: Mirall showing “Waiting to be let in” after joining a space, with the existing members' avatars and a Cancel request button

blocks.text: Open the space and click **Invite**. The dialog asks you two things before it mints a link:

items: **Auto-approve** — off by default. Leave it off and everyone who uses the link asks to join, and you approve each one. Turn it on and anyone holding the link is admitted instantly.
**Link expires after** — **2 hours**, **2 days**, or **2 weeks**. After that the link stops working and Mirall refuses anyone who tries to use it.

blocks.text: Click **Create invite link**. Mirall shows a single `mirall://join/…` link, tagged with the choices you made, and a **Copy** button. Send it however you like — chat, email, anything. The same link works for as many people as you want to admit, until it expires. **Change** takes you back to adjust the settings; **Done** closes the dialog.
The link carries the space name, so the person joining sees what they're joining.

image.alt: Mirall's Invite to Space dialog with an auto-approve toggle and a link expiry choice of 2 hours, 2 days, or 2 weeks

docs.title: Approve or deny join requests
docs.intro: When someone uses an invite that isn't set to auto-approve, they stay outside the space until a member lets them in. Any member can do this — not just whoever created the space.

blocks.text: A banner appears at the top of the space reading **“<name> wants to join”**, with **Approve** and **Deny** beside it. Approving hands them the key to the space's contents; denying turns them away.

When several people are waiting, the banner collapses into a stack of avatars and a count — **“3 people want to join”** — with a **Review** button. That opens **Requests to join**, where you can tick individuals and **Approve selected**, take everyone at once with **Approve all**, or deny someone from their row.
A request only needs handling once. As soon as any member approves or denies someone, the banner clears for everyone else in the space.

image.alt: Mirall's “Requests to join” dialog listing people waiting to join, with Approve selected and Approve all buttons

blocks.text: Star any space to pin it to **Favorites**. On the Shared Spaces screen you can filter the list by **All Spaces** or **Favorites**. You can also add a space to Favorites from the **More** menu inside the space.

Open the space, open the **More** menu, and choose **Leave Space**. Mirall cancels any in-flight transfers and drops the space's records from your device. Files you've already downloaded, and any folder you were mirroring, stay where they are — they're yours.
If you're waiting to be approved rather than already a member, the same button reads **Cancel request** and simply withdraws your request.

Drag files onto the **Drop to Share** zone in the sidebar, or use **Share…** to pick them from your computer. While you drag a file over the space, the drop zone grows to fill the whole area, so you don't have to aim carefully. Mirall offers the file to the space right away.
Mirall shares the file **in place**, straight from the original on your disk. Sharing never doubles a file's disk usage, and when you edit a shared file, the change is picked up where it lies.

There's no size limit. Hundreds of gigabytes, or a file into the terabytes, all work the same way — content is streamed in chunks rather than uploaded as a whole. Mirall does need a moment to index a file before sharing can start, so expect a short **Preparing** step on something that large. Members waiting on it can watch that preparation progress rather than staring at a blank spinner.
Want to share a whole folder instead of picking files? See **Share a whole folder**.

Click the download button on any file shared by another member. The file transfers directly from their device to yours, showing live progress, average speed, and an estimate of the time remaining. Downloads land in your chosen download folder.
When a download finishes, a green shield appears next to the file. That's Mirall confirming the contents it received match the owner's original exactly.

docs.title: Pause, resume, cancel, or retry a transfer

blocks.text: If your network drops or the member you're downloading from goes offline, the transfer pauses on its own and picks back up from where it left off once that person is back. You can also take control by hand — the buttons on a file row change with its state:

items: **Pause Download** — stop a running download. Its progress is kept, so resuming costs you nothing.
**Cancel** — stop a running download outright and throw away what's been received.
**Resume** — continue a paused download from where it stopped.
**Discard Partial** — throw away a download that's paused or stalled and free the space it was using.
**Retry** — reattempt a download that ended in an error; **Dismiss** clears the failed row instead.

blocks.text: In-progress downloads are written to a `.mirall.part` file and renamed to their real name only when the transfer completes — an interrupted transfer can't leave a half-written file under its final name.
A mirrored folder has no per-file download controls. To pause or stop everything a folder is pulling, use the mirror's own **Pause Mirror**, **Resume Mirror**, and **Unmount Mirror** actions.

related.label: Pause, resume, move, or stop a mirror

docs.title: See who's downloading from you
docs.intro: For any file you share, Mirall shows you who is pulling it right now.

blocks.text: While someone downloads a file you've shared, their avatar appears on that file's row, along with a combined progress bar, the transfer speed, and an estimate of the time remaining. If several people are downloading at once, their avatars stack up with a count.

Click the row's indicator to expand it. You'll see each person individually — their name, how far along they are, their own speed, and their own time remaining. Someone who has paused shows as **Paused**; someone who has dropped offline mid-transfer shows as **Waiting**.
This works for loose files in a space and for files inside a folder you share. It only appears on files you own — you don't see other people's downloads from other people.

image.alt: A shared file in Mirall expanded to show each person downloading it, with their avatar, progress, speed, and time remaining

blocks.text: Use a file's menu and choose **Unshare from Space** to remove a file you added so it's no longer offered to other members. Files they've already downloaded stay on their devices — unsharing only stops offering new copies. Your own original is untouched.

docs.title: Share a whole folder
docs.intro: Share a folder into a space and Mirall keeps it in sync — add, edit, or remove files and everyone in the space sees the change automatically. Subfolders come along too.

items.text: Open the space you want to share into.
Open **Add Folder** from **Share…** in the sidebar, the **File** menu, or `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + U`.
Choose a folder on your computer with **Browse…** and give it a **share name** — how it appears to other members. The name must be unique within the space.
Click **Next: Preview**. Mirall scans the folder and shows what it's about to share — the file count and total size.
Click **Add Folder**. Mirall starts offering the folder's files. Changes you make to the folder later sync to the space automatically.

blocks.text: Folders you share are marked **Shared by you**. Mirall serves the files straight from the originals, so sharing a folder never doubles its disk usage.

image.alt: Mirall's Add Folder dialog with a folder path, a share name field, and a Next: Preview button

blocks.text: Open a folder another member shared to see what's inside without downloading everything. A folder you're only looking through is marked **Browse**.

Subfolders are rows you can open and close. Each one tells you what's inside before you open it: how many files it holds and their total size, how many are already on your device, how many are downloading right now, and how many subfolders it contains. **Expand all** opens the whole tree at once, and flips to **Collapse all** to fold it back up.
Click **Download** on any individual file to save just that one, or use **Mirror to Disk…** to keep the whole folder synced instead.
Folders you leave open stay open as you move around the app. Restarting Mirall resets the tree to its default, with the top-level folders open.

image.alt: A shared folder in Mirall browsed as a tree, with subfolders showing file counts and sizes and an Expand all button

docs.title: Mirror a folder to disk
docs.intro: Keep a live, always-up-to-date copy of a shared folder in a folder on your own computer.

items.text: Open the shared folder and choose **Mirror to Disk…**.
Pick a location on your computer with **Browse…**, then click **Next: Preview** to see what will be downloaded.
Click **Start Mirroring**. Mirall downloads the contents and keeps them in sync as the owner makes changes.

blocks.text: A mirror is **read-only**. If you edit a file inside the mirror, Mirall restores it to the owner's version on the next sync. To keep your own edits, copy the file out of the mirror folder first.

A mirror can't be placed directly on a top-level personal folder such as Home, Desktop, Documents, or Downloads. If the mirror's folder already holds a file of your own with the same name as a shared one, your file is left alone and the mirrored copy lands under a different name.

image.alt: Mirall's Mirror to Disk dialog, picking a location for a read-only copy that stays in sync with the owner's folder

docs.title: Pause, resume, move, or stop a mirror

items: **Pause Mirror** — stop syncing for now; the folder stays where it is and won't update until you resume.
**Resume Mirror** — start syncing again from wherever the owner's copy is now.
**Change Mirror Location…** — move the mirror to a different folder on your computer. This one lives on the folder's card in the space view.
**Unmount Mirror** — stop mirroring entirely and remove the mirrored copy. The folder reverts to **Browse**.

blocks.text: If a write fails — a full disk, or a destination that's become read-only — the mirror pauses rather than erroring, and resumes once there's room.

image.alt: A mirrored folder in Mirall with every file marked “On your device” and confirmed by a green verified shield

docs.title: See who's mirroring a folder you shared
docs.intro: A folder you own shows you the people keeping a live copy of it.

blocks.text: Open a folder you share and look for **Mirrored by** in the sidebar. One person shows by name with their current state underneath. Several stack into a row of avatars with a summary beneath — for example **“2 Syncing… · 1 Synced”**.

Each person is in one of three states: **Synced** (their copy matches yours), **Syncing…** (they're still catching up), or **Paused** (they've paused their mirror). The states stay accurate even while those people are offline.

image.alt: The Mirrored by panel on a shared folder in Mirall, showing the people keeping a live copy and whether each is synced, syncing, or paused

blocks.text: Open **Settings → Storage**. The **Download Folder** section at the top shows where downloads land; **Change…** lets you pick any folder via your OS picker. Every download from every space goes there from then on, and the choice persists across restarts. It defaults to your OS Downloads folder.

docs.title: See how much disk space Mirall uses

blocks.text: Open **Settings → Storage** and look at **App Storage**. This is what Mirall itself occupies on your device — not your files. Because Mirall shares your files in place, it never keeps a second copy of them, so this figure stays small.
**Show details** breaks it into two parts:

items: **Shared-file index** — compact maps of the files you share, never their contents. Freed automatically when you stop sharing a file.
**App database** — your spaces, members, and sync history.

image.alt: Mirall's Storage settings showing the download folder and the App Storage breakdown into a shared-file index and app database

blocks.text: Mirall tidies up after itself as it goes, but you can prompt it. In **Settings → Storage**, open **Show details** under App Storage and click **Free up space**. Mirall compacts its own bookkeeping and reports how much it recovered — or tells you storage is already optimized.
This only touches Mirall's own data. Your shared files, your downloads, and your mirrored folders are never removed.

Click your avatar in the top-right to open the **Account** page. Under **Profile** you can change your display name and avatar, then click **Save Changes** — the update propagates to every space you're in. Your profile is only shared with the peers you connect with. Avatars must be under 16 MB.

docs.title: Check how your identity is protected

blocks.text: Your identity in Mirall is a signing key held on your device. The **Security** section of the **Account** page tells you how that key is being kept safe:

items: **Your identity key is protected by the system keychain** — the normal case. The key is wrapped using your operating system's secure keychain, so copying Mirall's data folder doesn't hand anyone your identity.
**No system keychain is available** — Mirall couldn't reach a keychain on this machine, so the key falls back on your disk encryption. Turning on full-disk encryption is worth doing here.
**Identity-key protection is disabled** — protection isn't active on this install.

image.alt: Mirall's Account page showing the Security section confirming the identity key is protected by the system keychain

blocks.text: Your avatar gains a soft pulsing ring when something's wrong — red if you've lost internet, amber while Mirall is trying to recover. No ring means you're connected and ready. When a problem appears, a toast notification offers a **Show details** link.

Open **Account → Network** for a friendly diagnostics view — useful when a friend can't reach you or a transfer stalls. It explains what Mirall sees about your connection, points out things worth checking (router, VPN, firewall), and includes a **Reconnect** button to nudge things along.

Mirall lives in your menu bar (macOS) or system tray (Windows and Linux). Closing the window keeps the app running so peers stay connected, transfers keep going, and notifications still arrive — click the icon to bring the window back.
**Settings → General** lets you opt out: **Show Mirall in the menu bar** on macOS, **Run in the background** on Windows and Linux. **Launch at login** starts Mirall quietly with your computer.
To quit Mirall outright rather than close it to the tray, use **File → Quit Mirall** (`Ctrl + Q`) on Windows and Linux, or **Mirall → Quit** (`Cmd + Q`) on macOS.

Mirall ships in five languages — English, German, French, Spanish, and Italian. Your OS locale is detected on first launch. Switch any time under **Settings → Appearance → Language**; your choice persists across restarts.

docs.title: Change the theme and display size

blocks.text: Under **Settings → Appearance**, pick a **Theme Mode** — Light, System, or Dark (System follows your OS automatically). Set the **Display Size** to Compact, Cozy, Default, or Spacious, or step through any zoom level with `Cmd/Ctrl +`, `−`, and `0`. Your choice persists across restarts.
On Windows and Linux, **Auto-hide the menu bar** tucks the menu bar away until you press `Alt`. Keyboard shortcuts keep working either way.

Mirall surfaces incoming files and space activity through your operating system's notification center. Open **Settings → Notifications**. Three switches cover the basics: **Show desktop notifications** (the master switch), **Play sound**, and **Mute when Mirall is focused**, which skips alerts while the window is in front.
Under **Events**, five per-event toggles let you opt in or out individually:

items: **Someone comes online** — a member of one of your spaces appears online.
**Someone goes offline** — a member of one of your spaces disconnects.
**Download finished** — a file you're receiving has finished downloading.
**Download failed** — a download could not be completed.
**Download paused** — a download paused because the sender went offline.

blocks.text: Requests to join a space always surface in the app itself, as a banner on the space and a notice you can act on. They don't depend on these settings.
You don't have to do anything — Mirall checks for new versions on its own and downloads them in the background. A staged update is applied the next time you quit and reopen Mirall, on your schedule.

When an update is waiting, a banner appears at the top of the window (you can dismiss it), and the pending version is also shown on the **Settings → About** screen so you can always check what's coming.

reference.label: Reference
reference.heading: Reference
reference.intro: Complete, lookup-able facts about Mirall — statuses, types, shortcuts, settings, and menus.

sections.title: File statuses
sections.intro: What each label on a file means. Colour carries meaning: green when a file is on your device, blue while it's moving, yellow when it needs your attention, red for an error, and grey when it's offered but not yet yours.

rows: A file you added to the space. It stays where it is on your disk and is offered to other members.
A file you downloaded from another member. It's yours to keep and works offline.
A transfer is in progress, showing progress, speed, and time remaining.
The download finished and Mirall is checking the contents match the owner's original.
The owner is still indexing this file. You can watch their progress while you wait.
A file you just added is being indexed so other members can fetch it.
A download stopped — because you paused it, or because it was interrupted. Resume to continue.
A transfer errored out. Retry to try again, or dismiss it.
The member who shared this file is online, so you can pull it. Click download.
The member who shared this file is offline. The download resumes on its own once they're back.
The file can't be obtained right now.

sections.title: The verified shield
sections.intro: A green shield next to a finished download means Mirall checked the file's contents against the owner's original and they match exactly. It appears only on a completed download, never on a file you shared yourself.

sections.title: Folder & share badges
sections.intro: The badge on a shared folder tells you your relationship to it.

rows: A folder on your computer that you share into the space. Your local changes sync to the space automatically.
A folder another member shares that you're looking through. Download individual files, or mirror the whole folder.
A folder another member shares that you keep as a live, read-only copy on your disk. It stays in sync as the owner changes it.
A mirror whose syncing you've paused. Files won't update until you resume it.
A folder you share whose source folder has been moved or deleted. Point Mirall at its new location to reconnect it.

sections.title: The space sidebar
sections.intro: Each space view has a sidebar with a Drop to Share zone plus two panels — Space Storage and Members — that you can collapse to fold away what you're not using.

image.alt: A Mirall space sidebar with the Space Storage panel collapsed and the Members panel expanded

sections.title: File & folder actions
list: **Reveal in Folder** — open a downloaded or shared file in your OS file browser.
**Unshare from Space** — remove a file you added so it's no longer offered (files others already downloaded stay on their devices).
**Pause Download** / **Resume** — stop a running download and continue it later from where it stopped.
**Cancel** — stop a running download outright and throw away what's been received.
**Discard Partial** — throw away a paused or stalled download and free the space it was using.
**Retry** / **Dismiss** — reattempt a download that ended in an error, or clear the failed row.
**Mirror to Disk…** — keep a live, read-only copy of a shared folder on your computer.
**Pause Mirror** / **Resume Mirror** — stop or restart a mirror's syncing.
**Change Mirror Location…** — move a mirror to a different folder.
**Unmount Mirror** — stop mirroring and remove the mirrored copy; the folder reverts to Browse.
**Free up space** — compact Mirall's own bookkeeping, in Settings → Storage.

sections.title: Keyboard shortcuts
sections.intro: Press ⌘K / Ctrl-K to jump to any space or run any action; press ⌘/ / Ctrl-/ for this list inside the app.

rows: Go back to the previous screen
Join a space with an invite
Add files to the current space
Share a folder in the current space

sections.title: Getting around
sections.intro: Move through Mirall the way you would in a browser.
list: **Back** — your mouse's back button, a two-finger swipe on macOS, `Cmd/Ctrl + ←`, or **View → Back**.
**Home** — click the Mirall logo, or press `Ctrl-H` (`Cmd-Shift-H` on macOS) to return to your Shared Spaces.
**Command palette** — `Cmd/Ctrl + K` to search and jump to any space or run any action.
**Invite to this space** and **Leave this space** are reachable from the command palette while a space is open, and have no key of their own.

sections.title: Settings
sections.intro: The gear icon opens Settings — everything about the app itself. Your profile, security status, and network connection live separately on the Account page (click your avatar).

rows: Background mode — “Show Mirall in the menu bar” on macOS, “Run in the background” on Windows and Linux — and Launch at login.
Theme Mode (Light / System / Dark), Display Size, Language, and — on Windows and Linux — auto-hide the menu bar.
Master switch, system sound, mute-when-focused, and five per-event toggles.
Download folder, and Mirall's own App Storage — a shared-file index plus the app database — with a Free up space action.
Installed version, any pending update, keyboard shortcuts, documentation and feedback links, and What's new.

sections.title: The Account page
sections.intro: Click your avatar in the top right.

rows: Your display name and avatar. Changes propagate to every space you're in.
How your identity key is protected — normally by your operating system's keychain.
Whether you're online and how many peers you're connected to. Opens a fuller diagnostics screen.

sections.title: Application menu
sections.intro: Available on Windows and Linux as well as macOS. Items that act on a space are enabled only while one is open.

rows: About Mirall; Preferences… (⌘,); Hide / Hide Others / Show All; Quit.
New Space (⌘/Ctrl-N); Join Space (⌘/Ctrl-J); Add Files… (⌘/Ctrl-U); Add Folder… (⌘/Ctrl-Shift-U); Invite People…. On Windows and Linux, also Preferences… (Ctrl-,) and Quit Mirall (Ctrl-Q).
The standard Undo, Redo, Cut, Copy, Paste, and Select All.
Back; Home; Command Palette; Keyboard Shortcuts; zoom in, out, and reset; full screen.
What's New…; Documentation; Send Feedback…. On Windows and Linux, also About Mirall.

sections.title: Platforms & languages
list: **Platforms** — macOS, Windows, and Linux (x64 and ARM64).
**Languages** — English, German, French, Spanish, and Italian, auto-detected from your OS on first launch.
**Updates** — delivered automatically in the background and applied when you next quit and reopen.
**File sizes** — no limit. Files are streamed in chunks, so hundreds of gigabytes or a file into the terabytes transfer like any other.

explanation.label: Explanation
explanation.heading: Explanation
explanation.intro: Background and mental models — the why behind how Mirall behaves.

topics.title: How Mirall works: peer-to-peer, no server

blocks.text: Mirall connects your devices directly. When you share a file, its contents stay on your device — right where they already are — and other members download it straight from you over an encrypted connection. There is no cloud server in the middle storing or relaying your files.

What does travel ahead of time is lightweight **metadata** — names, sizes, and which member is offering what — so everyone can see what's available in a space. The actual bytes of a file move only when someone chooses to download it.

The corollary is that availability follows people, not servers. A file is downloadable only while the member who shared it is online. Downloading a file gives you your own copy to keep — but it doesn't turn you into a second source for it, so the person who shared it stays the one who serves it.

Every connection between devices is end-to-end encrypted, so nobody can see your files or metadata in transit, and there's no central server that could be breached. There are no accounts and no tracking.
Getting in is not the same as being able to read

A space's contents are protected by a key of their own. That key is handed to you only once an existing member approves your request to join. Reaching a space, or holding a link to it, is therefore not the same as being able to read what's shared in it — a pending member is outside the door, not merely unlisted.
Your device at rest

Mirall's local bookkeeping — your list of spaces, mirrored folders, downloads, and similar details — is encrypted on your device with a key tied to your identity. Your identity itself is a signing key, wrapped using your operating system's secure keychain. Copying Mirall's data folder off your machine gives up neither your records nor your identity. The **Security** section of the Account page tells you which protection is in force.
Unfriendly peers

Because peers talk to each other directly, Mirall assumes some of them may misbehave. Connection rate limits, size caps, and a firewall for badly-behaved peers keep a flood of requests or oversized data from exhausting your memory or CPU. Oversized avatars and over-long display names sent by a peer are rejected rather than rendered.
Your profile — display name and avatar — is shared only with the peers you actually connect with.

Joining a space is a **request**, not an entitlement. Someone who follows your invite link becomes a pending member: they're visible to the space, but they hold none of its contents and can take no member-only action until an existing member approves them. Approving them is what hands over the key.

**Any** member can approve, not just whoever created the space — and a request only needs answering once. As soon as one member approves or denies someone, that decision propagates and the request clears for everyone else.

If you'd rather not gate-keep, turn on **auto-approve** when you create the invite link. Anyone holding that link is then admitted the moment they use it. Either way the link carries an expiry — 2 hours, 2 days, or 2 weeks — after which Mirall refuses it.

The trade-off is deliberate. Auto-approve trades a moment of your attention for the risk that a forwarded link admits someone you didn't intend. Approval keeps that decision with the people already in the room.

A space is a private group with its own members and its own files. Because files live on members' devices rather than a server, a file is downloadable only while the member who shared it is **online**.

That member is the file's single source. Downloading a file gives you a copy that's yours to keep and works offline, but it doesn't make you a second source others can pull from — so if the person who shared it goes offline, the file is marked **Owner offline** until they're back, and any download in flight picks up where it left off.

Plan around this for anything time-critical: the person sharing a file needs to be online and running Mirall while someone downloads it. Mirall doesn't hand a file off to the rest of the space to keep serving in their absence.
When you share a file or a folder, Mirall serves it **in place** — straight from the original on your disk. It doesn't import a private copy into its own storage.

Two things follow from that. Sharing never doubles a file's disk usage, however large it is. And because there's no copy to fall out of date, an edit you make to a shared file is picked up where it lies, rather than needing to be re-imported.

What Mirall does keep is an **index**: a compact map of a file's contents that lets it stream the right pieces to whoever asks. That index is small, it never contains the file's contents, and it's freed as soon as you stop sharing the file.

The consequence to keep in mind: the original file is the shared file. Move it, rename it, or delete it, and there is no second copy standing behind it. A folder whose source has gone is flagged **Folder is missing on disk** until you point Mirall at its new location.
When you mirror a shared folder to disk, you get a live copy that tracks the owner's folder. The owner is the single source of truth, which is why a mirror is **read-only**.

If you change a file inside a mirror, the next sync restores it to the owner's version — your edit is discarded. This keeps every mirror faithful to the original. To work on a file, copy it out of the mirror folder first; to propose changes back, share them with the owner separately.

A mirror only ever manages the files it put there. A file of your own that happens to sit in the same folder is left alone — including when the owner deletes the shared file it collided with. For the same reason, a mirror can't be placed directly on a top-level personal folder like Home, Desktop, Documents, or Downloads.

From the other side, a folder you own shows you who is mirroring it and whether each person is synced, still catching up, or paused — so you can tell whether the people who need your files actually have them.
There are three distinct things on your device, and only one of them is Mirall's:

items: **Your files** — the originals you share. Mirall reads them where they are and never copies them.
**Files you've pulled** — downloads, and the contents of folders you mirror. These are ordinary files in ordinary folders that you chose. They're yours; Mirall doesn't reclaim them.
**Mirall's own data** — the shared-file index and the app database (your spaces, members, and sync history). This is the **App Storage** figure in Settings → Storage.

blocks.text: Only the third is Mirall's to manage, and it stays small precisely because there's no second copy of your files inside it. Mirall tidies it as it goes; **Free up space** prompts it to compact things now and tells you what it recovered.
Mirall checks for new versions on its own and downloads them in the background while you keep working — no installer downloads, no reinstalls. Updates never restart the app on their own.
A staged update is applied the next time you quit and reopen Mirall, so restarts happen on your schedule. You can see a pending version on the Settings → About screen before you restart.

changelog.label: Changelog
changelog.heading: What's new
changelog.intro: Notable changes you'll notice as a user. Internal refactors and build-pipeline tweaks are intentionally left out.

sections.heading: Added
items: **Share straight from your files — no second copy.** Mirall used to import a private copy of everything you shared into its own storage. It now serves your shared files and folders directly from the originals on your computer, so sharing no longer doubles their disk usage, and edits to a shared file are picked up in place.

**Approve who joins your spaces.** Joining a space is now a request: the person stays pending until an existing member approves them, and only then do they gain access to the space's contents. You'll see a "wants to join" banner and can approve or deny individually or in a batch; the person waiting sees a clear "waiting for approval" view. Prefer the old open behaviour? Flip on **auto-approve** when you create the invite and anyone with the code is admitted automatically.

**See who's downloading from you.** When someone downloads a file you've shared, its row now shows their avatar — expand it to see each person individually, with live progress, transfer speed, and an estimate of how long they have left.

**Live speed and time-remaining on transfers.** Downloads now show their average speed and a steady estimate of the time remaining. While you wait on a file, you can watch the owner's live preparation progress instead of a blank "Preparing…".

**Pause, resume, and stop transfers.** Downloads can be paused and resumed, stopped outright, and pick back up on their own when the owner comes back online. Each finished file carries a "verified" badge confirming its contents match the original exactly.

**Browse a shared folder as a collapsible tree.** Subfolders are now rows you can open and close, each telling you what's inside at a glance: how many files it holds, their total size, how many are already on your device, and how many are downloading right now. **Expand all** and **Collapse all** fold the whole tree at once.
**See who's mirroring a folder you shared.** A folder you own now shows the people keeping a live copy of it, each with their current state — synced, syncing, or paused.
**A bigger target for drag-and-drop sharing.** Dragging a file anywhere over a space now expands the drop zone to fill the whole area.
**Quit from the menu on Windows and Linux.** The File menu now offers "Quit Mirall" (Ctrl+Q), so you can fully quit the app instead of only closing it to the tray.

sections.heading: Security
items: **Your local app data is encrypted on your device.** Mirall's local bookkeeping — your list of spaces, mirrored folders, downloads, and similar details — is now encrypted at rest with a key tied to your identity, so copying Mirall's data folder no longer exposes it. Existing data is migrated automatically the first time you open this version.

**Only approved members can read a space.** A space's contents are now gated by a per-space key that's handed out only once an existing member approves your request to join — so being able to reach a space is no longer the same as being able to read what's shared in it.

**Your identity is encrypted on your device.** Your signing key is now wrapped using your operating system's secure keychain and the plaintext is removed from storage. The Account screen shows this protection status.

**Tougher against abusive peers.** Connection rate limits, sensible caps, and a firewall for misbehaving peers keep a flood of requests or oversized data from exhausting memory or CPU. Oversized avatars and over-long display names sent by a peer are now rejected.

sections.heading: Changed
items: **Simpler folder sharing.** Sharing a folder no longer asks you to pick a transfer mode. The previous "On demand" option, and the cache-size setting it added to Storage settings, have been removed — serving files in place from their originals covers the same need automatically, with no separate cache to manage.

**Clearer, more consistent status colors.** File status labels now follow a fixed, meaningful palette: green when a file is on your device, blue while it's transferring, yellow when something needs your attention, and red for an error.

**File sizes now match what your operating system shows.** Sizes were divided in binary (1024) steps but labelled with decimal units, so a file Finder reports as 629.68 GB could read as "586.4 GB". Sizes now use the same decimal units as macOS Finder and GNOME Files, and very large files no longer show "undefined".

**Invites now come only from inside the space.** Creating a space no longer hands you an invite code on the spot — it ends on a simple confirmation. To invite someone, open the space and use its **Invite** dialog: choose whether to auto-approve and how long the invite stays valid (2 hours, 2 days, or 2 weeks), then create a single link to share. Expired links are refused when someone tries to join.
**A native Mac build for each processor.** Mirall now ships separate downloads for Intel and Apple Silicon Macs, so each gets a build matched to its processor.

sections.heading: Fixed
items: **Shared files stay inside the shared folder.** Specially crafted file paths from a peer can no longer reach outside the folder you chose — neither to read or stream files from an owner's disk, nor to write or delete files on a mirror.

**Mirroring never overwrites your own files.** If a shared file has the same name as one you already have, the mirrored copy now lands under a non-colliding name, and a peer can only ever remove its own mirrored copy — never your original. A mirror also can't be placed directly on a top-level personal folder such as Home, Desktop, Documents, or Downloads.

**Member lists stay in sync for everyone.** We rebuilt how membership is shared between peers, so people no longer go missing, show up as "Unknown", or get stuck — and someone approved by one member now appears for everyone. Members also show their real name and avatar even before a direct connection is made.
**Leaving a space sticks, even if you quit part-way.** Quitting while a space was still being left could bring it back on next launch; the leave now completes instead of reappearing.

**Files and members catch up on slow or flaky connections without a restart.** On unreliable links a late-approved member might not see the space creator, and newly shared files could stay invisible until you restarted. Mirall now keeps re-checking in the background until everyone's view matches.
**Pasted invite links work in the Join dialog.** Copying the "app link" form of an invite and pasting it into Join now works, not just the bare code.

**Mirroring pauses instead of erroring when a write fails** — for example when your disk is full or the destination is read-only — and resumes once there's room, rather than retrying the failing file forever.
**Fixed a crash when showing a notification on Windows** for certain spaces, members, or transfers.

**Long names no longer overflow confirmation dialogs.** A long file, folder, or space name in a Remove, Leave, Delete, or Mirror dialog spilled out of the panel and pushed the close button aside. Names are now shortened in the middle, keeping the file extension readable.

sections.heading: Added
items: **Share whole folders, not just individual files.** Pick a folder on your computer to share into a space and Mirall keeps it in sync — add, edit, or remove files and everyone in the space sees the change automatically. Subfolders come along too.

**Browse and mirror folders others share.** Open a shared folder to see what's inside without downloading everything, or mirror it to a folder on your own computer to keep a live, always-up-to-date copy. Pause, resume, or stop mirroring whenever you want.

**Share a folder "on demand".** A toggle in the Add Folder dialog offers a folder's files to the space but only sends each one when someone actually downloads it — handy for large folders you don't want to push up front.
**The application menu is now on Windows and Linux,** not just macOS.
**See and free up space per shared folder.** Storage settings now shows how much each shared folder is using, and Mirall reclaims unused space on its own when a space goes quiet.
**Collapsible Storage and Members panels** in the space sidebar, so you can fold away what you're not using.

sections.heading: Changed
items: **A more consistent interface throughout.** Status labels and colors, avatars, buttons, corner radii, and spacing have been harmonized across every screen.

**Quicker ways to get around.** Go back the way you would in a browser — your mouse's back button, a two-finger swipe on macOS, or Cmd/Ctrl+Left — and jump back to your spaces anytime from the logo or with Ctrl+H (Cmd+Shift+H on Mac).
**Full keyboard and screen-reader support.** Every screen is now navigable by keyboard and works with assistive technology, and Mirall honors your system's reduced-motion preference.
**A pending update now shows in the About screen,** and the update banner no longer covers up content while you work.
**Fewer prompts when installing on Windows.** The installer no longer asks for permissions Mirall never actually needed.

sections.heading: Fixed
items: **Downloads no longer overwrite your existing files.** A download whose name matched one already in your downloads folder now lands at a safe, non-colliding name.
**Space members no longer go missing.** Joining a busy space could occasionally drop someone from your member list until you reconnected — everyone now shows up reliably.
**Windows updates now install reliably,** resolving a failure that could stop a detected update from applying.
**Re-adding a file at the same path is handled correctly** instead of looking like a stuck, half-finished download.
**No more flicker** when you drag a file over the drop zone.
**A cancelled download no longer leaves a row stuck as "paused".**
**The "on your device" indicator stays accurate** after a file is removed or re-shared.
**The file list stays usable** when no peers are currently connected.

sections.heading: Fixed
items: **Windows updates now install.** New versions were detected and the banner appeared, but on Windows the update never actually applied. Updates now install correctly on quit and restart.
**The version number shows immediately in the About screen** instead of briefly displaying a placeholder while it loaded.

sections.heading: Fixed
items: **Scrollbars match the app again.** They had reverted to the pale system default; scrollbars now follow the theme across all platforms.

sections.heading: Fixed
items: **Invite code shown after creating a space now matches the one in the Invite dialog,** and carries the space name so the person joining sees the real name instead of "Shared Space".

sections.heading: Added
items: **Shareable invite links.** Send an invite as a clickable link that opens Mirall straight to the Join dialog, or as the plain code. Invites also carry the space name.
**New Account page.** Click your avatar in the top-right to open a dedicated page that gathers your profile and your network connection in one place.
**At-a-glance connection status.** Your avatar gains a soft pulsing ring when something's wrong — red if you've lost internet, amber while Mirall is trying to recover.
**A heads-up when your connection drops.** A toast appears the moment Mirall notices a problem, with a Show details link to the Network page.
**Network page with friendly diagnostics** — what Mirall sees about your connection, things worth checking, and a Reconnect button.

sections.heading: Changed
items: **Settings is now just app settings.** Profile editing and network status moved to the new Account page. Settings keeps Appearance, General, Notifications, Storage, and About.

sections.heading: Fixed
items: **No more empty files from dropped folders.** Dragging a folder used to silently create an empty file; Mirall now recognises the folder and asks you to pick individual files instead.

sections.heading: Added
items: **Mirall now lives in your menu bar (macOS) or system tray (Windows / Linux).** Closing the window no longer shuts the app down — peers stay connected and transfers keep going. Settings → General lets you opt out, or opt in to Launch at login.
**Keyboard shortcuts and a command palette.** Press ⌘K (Ctrl-K) for a command palette, and ⌘/ (Ctrl-/) for the full shortcut list.

sections.heading: Changed
items: **Settings reorganized.** Theme, display size, and language now live under a new Appearance page; background mode and Launch at login under General.

sections.heading: Fixed
items: **No more duplicate "online" notifications** when a peer's connection briefly drops and comes back.
**No more white flash when resizing the window** — the window background now tracks your chosen theme.

sections.heading: Fixed
items: **More reliable first launch.** Mirall now starts up cleanly even while a newer version is being fetched in the background.
**Smoother updates on Windows and Linux** — a new version installs quietly in the background and is live the next time you quit and reopen.
**Better contrast** on the Downloading status pill in the light theme, meeting WCAG contrast requirements.

sections.heading: Added
items: **Choose where downloads go.** Storage settings has a new Download folder entry; pick any folder and every download lands there.
**Clear peer cache (per space)** from a kebab menu on each Storage row, without leaving the space.
**Notifications when a peer removes a shared file** you're downloading, instead of the transfer hanging silently.

sections.heading: Changed
items: **Honest per-space storage figures** — downloaded files now count against the space they belong to.
**Atomic downloads.** In-progress downloads write to a hidden .partial file and rename on completion.

sections.heading: Fixed
items: Transfer-failed notifications now show a translated reason instead of a raw error code.

sections.heading: Added
items: **"What's new" modal.** After an update, Mirall shows what changed; the full history is also under Settings → About → What's new.
**Italian interface,** alongside German, English, French, and Spanish.
**Zoom levels** — Compact, Cozy, Default, and Spacious under Appearance, or step through with Cmd/Ctrl +, −, and 0.

sections.heading: Changed
items: Refreshed the light and dark themes — warmer per-space colors and better legibility in dark mode.

sections.heading: Fixed
items: Linux: the AppImage stays executable after an automatic update.

sections.heading: Added
items: **Desktop notifications** for incoming files and space activity.
**French and Spanish interface,** alongside German and English.
Styled scrollbars in the space and file lists.

sections.heading: Changed
items: Text selection is disabled app-wide for a native feel; copyable values now have dedicated copy buttons.

sections.heading: Fixed
items: **Screen reader support** — icon-only buttons and clickable rows now expose proper accessible names.

sections.heading: Added
items: **German interface,** auto-detected from your OS locale on first run.

sections.heading: Fixed
items: The file list now refreshes when a peer sharing files disconnects.
Windows: the update banner now appears as expected when a new build is available.
Storage settings: a clearer Cache / Metadata / share-of-total breakdown.

sections.heading: Added
items: Initial public release.
**Peer-to-peer file sharing** — files travel directly between you and the people you share with, with no central server.
**Spaces** — named collections with their own membership and invite codes.
**Built-in updates** that apply automatically.
Onboarding flow for choosing a display name and avatar.
Available for macOS, Windows, and Linux.
